en.extract:
The Natal spurfowl or Natal francolin (Pternistis natalensis) is a species of bird in the family Phasianidae.
It is found in Botswana, Eswatini, Mozambique, South Africa, Zambia, and Zimbabwe.


Taxonomy
The Natal spurfowl was described in 1833 by the Scottish zoologist Andrew Smith and given the binomial name Francolinus natalensis. Smith noted that the species inhabited brushwood thickets in the vicinity of Natal, in the east of South Africa. The species is now placed in the genus Pternistis that was introduced by the German naturalist Johann Georg Wagler in 1832. A phylogenetic study published in 2019 found that the Natal spurfowl is sister to Hildebrandt's spurfowl. The Natal spurfowl is considered as monotypic: the proposed subspecies neavei is not recognised.


References


External links

(Natal spurfowl = ) Natal francolin - Text and species map from Southern African Bird Atlas Project
Audio recordings of the Natal spurfowl on Xeno-canto.

The '''Natal spurfowl''' or '''Natal francolin''' ('''''Pternistis natalensis''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Phasianidae]].
It is found in [[Botswana]], [[Eswatini]], [[Mozambique]], [[South Africa]], [[Zambia]], and [[Zimbabwe]].

==Taxonomy==
The Natal spurfowl was described in 1833 by the Scottish zoologist [[Andrew Smith (zoologist)|Andrew Smith]] and given the [[binomial name]] ''Francolinus natalensis''. Smith noted that the species inhabited brushwood thickets in the vicinity of [[Colony of Natal|Natal]], in the east of South Africa.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Smith | first=Andrew | author-link=Andrew Smith (zoologist) | year=1833 | title=''Francolinus natalensis'' | journal=South African Quarterly Journal | volume=2 | page=48 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/15968164 }}</ref> The species is now placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Pternistis]]'' that was introduced by the German naturalist [[Johann Georg Wagler]] in 1832.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Wagler | first=Johann Georg | author-link=Johann Georg Wagler | year=1832 | title=Neue Sippen und Gattungen der Säugthiere und Vögel | journal=Isis von Oken | at=cols 1218–1235 [1229] | language=German, Latin | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/26455534 }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | year=2020 | title=Pheasants, partridges, francolins | work=IOC World Bird List Version 10.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/pheasants/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=14 February 2020 }}</ref> A phylogenetic study published in 2019 found that the Natal spurfowl is [[sister species|sister]] to [[Hildebrandt's spurfowl]].<ref name=mandiwana>{{ cite journal | last1=Mandiwana-Neudani | first1=T.G. | last2=Little | first2=R.M. | last3=Crowe | first3=T.M. | last4=Bowie | first4=R.C.K. | year=2019 | title=Taxonomy, phylogeny and biogeography of African spurfowls Galliformes, Phasianidae, Phasianinae, Coturnicini: ''Pternistis'' spp. | journal=Ostrich | volume=90 | issue=2 | pages=145–172 | doi=10.2989/00306525.2019.1584925 }}</ref> The Natal spurfowl is considered as [[monotypic]]: the proposed [[subspecies]] ''neavei'' is not recognised.<ref name=ioc/>
